Keywords: activation, first value, onboarding, aha moment, first action
Definition: Time from signup to when a user completes the action that delivers core product value
Formula / Measurement: Median time from account_created event to activation_event. Track in product analytics (Mixpanel/Amplitude)
Target Benchmark: <10 minutes for consumer SaaS; <1 day for complex B2B
Why It Matters: TTFV is the single strongest predictor of long-term retention. Every minute of friction before value delivery costs conversion.
Anti-Metric Warning: Don't use 'completed onboarding tour' as proxy — measure the actual value moment (first report generated, first contact imported, first task created)

Keywords: activate, onboard, complete, setup, first time
Definition: Percentage of signups who complete the core activation action within a defined window
Formula / Measurement: (Users who reach activation event within 7 days) / (Total signups in same cohort) × 100
Target Benchmark: 30-60% for consumer PLG; 15-40% for complex B2B
Why It Matters: Activation is the first conversion gate. Low activation means users leave before seeing value — no retention or monetization is possible.
Anti-Metric Warning: Don't use login as the activation event. It must be an action that delivers actual product value.

Keywords: mrr, revenue, recurring, subscription, arr
Definition: Total predictable recurring revenue per month from all active paying customers
Formula / Measurement: Sum of (plan price × number of customers on each plan). Exclude one-time fees.
Target Benchmark: Growing 10-20%+ MoM for early stage; 5-10% for growth stage
Why It Matters: MRR is the health metric for subscription businesses. Tracks whether the business is growing predictably.
Anti-Metric Warning: MRR growth without looking at the source (expansion vs new vs reactivation) is misleading. Always decompose.

Keywords: nrr, retention, expansion, upsell, churn, contraction
Definition: How much revenue from existing customers grew or shrank over a period — includes expansions and churned accounts
Formula / Measurement: (Starting MRR + Expansion MRR - Contraction MRR - Churned MRR) / Starting MRR × 100
Target Benchmark: 110%+ is world-class; 100%+ means growth without new customers; <90% is a red flag
Why It Matters: NRR > 100% means the business grows purely from its existing customer base. It's the single most important SaaS health metric at scale.
Anti-Metric Warning: Don't confuse with Gross Revenue Retention (GRR) which excludes expansion. Both matter.

Keywords: churn, cancel, lose, turnover, attrition, cancel rate
Definition: Percentage of customers who cancel or don't renew in a period
Formula / Measurement: (Customers lost in period) / (Total customers at start of period) × 100
Target Benchmark: <2% monthly for SMB SaaS; <5% annually for enterprise
Why It Matters: High churn destroys LTV and makes growth nearly impossible — you're pouring water into a leaky bucket.
Anti-Metric Warning: Monthly churn of 2% compounds to ~22% annually. Always track cohort churn, not just point-in-time.

Keywords: dau, wau, active users, engagement, usage, sticky
Definition: Number of unique users who perform a meaningful action on a given day or week
Formula / Measurement: Count of unique user_id who triggered at least one meaningful_event in the period
Target Benchmark: Depends on use case: daily use (Slack, Notion) vs weekly (project tools) vs monthly (analytics dashboards)
Why It Matters: Engagement frequency signals product-market fit for consumer products. Low DAU/MAU ratio means users aren't forming habits.
Anti-Metric Warning: Don't count login as an active event. Define 'meaningful action' based on your core value delivery.

Keywords: sticky, stickiness, habit, frequency, engagement, ratio
Definition: Ratio of daily active users to monthly active users — how 'sticky' the product is
Formula / Measurement: (DAU) / (MAU) × 100
Target Benchmark: 50%+ is excellent (WhatsApp ~70%); 20-30% is good; <10% is low stickiness
Why It Matters: Stickiness predicts long-term retention. A sticky product becomes a habit, which makes it very hard to churn.
Anti-Metric Warning: Stickiness depends on use case. A monthly billing tool with 15% DAU/MAU isn't 'low engagement' — don't compare across different use case types.

Keywords: convert, upgrade, paid, freemium, trial, plg, revenue conversion
Definition: Percentage of free users who convert to a paid plan in a given cohort window
Formula / Measurement: (Free users who upgrade to paid within N days) / (Total free signups in same cohort) × 100
Target Benchmark: 2-5% for open freemium; 15-25% for free trial with expiry
Why It Matters: For PLG companies, free-to-paid conversion is the revenue engine. Even a 1% improvement at scale has massive ARR impact.
Anti-Metric Warning: Increasing free-to-paid conversion by degrading the free product (paywalling too aggressively) hurts word-of-mouth and long-term growth.

Keywords: nps, promoter, detractor, recommend, satisfaction, loyalty
Definition: How likely users are to recommend the product to a colleague, on a 0-10 scale
Formula / Measurement: Survey: 'How likely are you to recommend us?' (0-10). NPS = % Promoters (9-10) - % Detractors (0-6)
Target Benchmark: 50+ is excellent; 30-50 is good; <0 means more detractors than promoters
Why It Matters: NPS predicts organic growth (referrals) and renewal risk (detractors). Read the qualitative comments for actionable insight.
Anti-Metric Warning: NPS is a lagging indicator — by the time it drops, churn has already started. Combine with in-product behavioral signals.

Keywords: cac, acquisition, cost, marketing, sales, spend
Definition: Total cost to acquire one new paying customer
Formula / Measurement: (Total Sales + Marketing spend in period) / (New customers acquired in period)
Target Benchmark: For B2B SaaS: CAC payback < 12 months; for enterprise: < 24 months
Why It Matters: If CAC exceeds LTV, the business is unsustainable. CAC payback period determines how quickly you can reinvest in growth.
Anti-Metric Warning: Blended CAC hides channel efficiency. Always track by channel (paid, organic, referral, outbound) to optimize spend.

Keywords: ltv, lifetime, value, revenue, retention, arpu
Definition: Total revenue expected from a customer over their entire relationship with the product
Formula / Measurement: LTV = ARPU × (1 / Monthly Churn Rate) or LTV = ARPU × Gross Margin / Monthly Churn Rate
Target Benchmark: LTV:CAC ratio > 3:1 is healthy; > 5:1 is excellent
Why It Matters: LTV:CAC is the fundamental unit economics ratio. It tells you whether acquiring customers is a profitable long-term business.
Anti-Metric Warning: LTV calculation assumes constant churn rate. For enterprise with multi-year contracts, use contract value instead.

Keywords: completion, onboarding, finish, course, setup, activate
Definition: Percentage of users who complete the full onboarding or course flow from start to finish
Formula / Measurement: (Users who reach final step / completion event) / (Users who started) × 100
Target Benchmark: 30-50% for complex multi-step onboarding; 60-80% for guided short flows
Why It Matters: Completion rate reveals where users drop off. Low completion means users don't reach value, which kills retention and expansion.
Anti-Metric Warning: Completion rate alone can be gamed by making onboarding too short to be skippable. Pair with downstream retention metrics.

Keywords: api, developer, adoption, calls, usage, integration
Definition: Percentage of signed-up developers who make at least one successful API call in their first 7 days
Formula / Measurement: (Developers who made ≥1 successful API call within 7 days) / (Total developer signups) × 100
Target Benchmark: 30-50%+ within 7 days for well-documented APIs
Why It Matters: API adoption is the activation metric for developer platforms. Low adoption means docs or onboarding is failing.
Anti-Metric Warning: Track time-to-first-successful-call (TTFC) as the leading indicator, not just whether adoption ever happened.

Keywords: feature, adoption, use, discover, activation, usage depth
Definition: Percentage of active users who have used a specific feature at least once in the past 30 days
Formula / Measurement: (MAU who triggered feature_used event) / (Total MAU) × 100
Target Benchmark: Varies by feature type. Core features: 60%+; Advanced features: 20-40%; Power user features: 5-15%
Why It Matters: Low feature adoption reveals discoverability and value clarity problems. High adoption validates product-market fit for that capability.
Anti-Metric Warning: Don't mistake low adoption for a dead feature — check if it's being used by the right personas (power users) before cutting it.

Keywords: support, ticket, resolution, customer success, response, sla
Definition: Average time from when a support ticket is opened to when it is resolved
Formula / Measurement: Median time from ticket_opened to ticket_resolved in your support system
Target Benchmark: < 4 hours for live chat; < 24 hours for email; < 4 hours for P0 enterprise SLA
Why It Matters: Support resolution time directly impacts NPS and renewal decisions. Enterprise customers have contractual SLA requirements.
Anti-Metric Warning: Median is more useful than average for this metric — outliers (complex escalations) will skew the average.

Keywords: audit, log, trail, compliance, security, enterprise, soc2
Definition: Percentage of sensitive user actions that are captured in the audit log
Formula / Measurement: (Logged sensitive actions) / (Total defined sensitive actions that should be logged) × 100
Target Benchmark: 100% for SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 compliance
Why It Matters: Enterprise buyers require audit logs for security review. SOC 2 auditors will verify coverage. Missing logs = compliance failure.
Anti-Metric Warning: Coverage rate doesn't tell you log quality — also verify that logs are queryable, exportable, and retained per policy.

Keywords: d1, d7, d30, retention, cohort, day 1, day 7, day 30, return, comeback, mobile retention
Definition: Percentage of users who return to the app on Day 1, Day 7, and Day 30 after first use
Formula / Measurement: (Users who open app on Day N) / (Users who installed on Day 0 in same cohort) × 100. Use Firebase or Amplitude cohort analysis.
Why It Matters: Retention curves are the single most important mobile metric. If D1 is low, onboarding failed. If D7 drops sharply, the core loop isn't engaging. If D30 is flat, you have product-market fit.
Anti-Metric Warning: Don't compare retention across app categories — a utility app (calculator) and a social app (Instagram) have fundamentally different natural usage frequencies.

Keywords: crash, stability, anr, error, bug, reliability, crash free, mobile stability
Definition: Percentage of app sessions that complete without a crash or ANR (Application Not Responding)
Formula / Measurement: (Total sessions - Sessions with crash/ANR) / (Total sessions) × 100. Track via Firebase Crashlytics or Sentry.
Target Benchmark: ≥99.5% is table stakes for App Store featuring; ≥99.9% is excellent; <99% requires immediate action
Why It Matters: Crashes directly impact app store ratings, user trust, and retention. Apple and Google use crash rates in store ranking algorithms. Every 0.1% improvement matters.
Anti-Metric Warning: Crash-free session rate hides crash-free user rate — 1 user crashing 50 times inflates session crash rate. Track both.

Keywords: app store, rating, review, stars, play store, ios rating, android rating, user review
Definition: Average star rating of the app on iOS App Store or Google Play Store
Formula / Measurement: Average of all user ratings. Weighted by recency (recent ratings matter more for store ranking).
Target Benchmark: ≥4.5 stars for top-tier apps; ≥4.0 is acceptable; <3.5 requires urgent attention
Why It Matters: App store rating directly affects download conversion rate (every 0.1 star matters), store search ranking, and brand perception. Apple features apps with 4.5+ ratings.
Anti-Metric Warning: Don't focus only on the average — read 1-star reviews for actionable issues. A 4.5 average with recurring complaints about one feature means that feature is broken for a segment.

Keywords: push, notification, opt-in, permission, allow, subscribe, fcm, apns, mobile notification
Definition: Percentage of app users who grant push notification permission
Formula / Measurement: (Users who granted push permission) / (Users who were prompted) × 100. Track via Firebase or OneSignal.
Target Benchmark: 60-70% opt-in with pre-permission screen; 40-50% without; iOS tends 10-15% lower than Android
Why It Matters: Push notifications are the #1 re-engagement channel for mobile apps. Low opt-in rate means you lose your primary tool for bringing users back. A well-designed pre-permission screen can increase opt-in by 30%.
Anti-Metric Warning: High opt-in is useless if you spam users — track notification-driven uninstalls alongside opt-in rate. Also track opt-out rate over time.

Keywords: cold start, launch time, startup, load time, performance, ttid, mobile performance
Definition: Time from user tapping the app icon to the first interactive screen being fully rendered
Formula / Measurement: Measure time from process creation to first meaningful paint (FMP). Use Firebase Performance Monitoring or custom trace.
Target Benchmark: <2s is good; <1s is excellent; >3s loses 20%+ of users. Apple recommends <400ms for 'instant' feel
Why It Matters: Every 100ms added to startup time reduces engagement. Users expect mobile apps to be instant. Slow cold start is the #1 cause of 'tried once, never came back'.
Anti-Metric Warning: Cold start on a developer's iPhone 15 Pro is not representative. Test on 3-year-old mid-range devices with limited RAM. Always measure P95, not average.

Keywords: session, length, duration, frequency, time in app, engagement, usage, daily sessions
Definition: Average time per session and average number of sessions per user per day/week
Formula / Measurement: Session length: median time from app_open to app_background/close. Frequency: sessions per user per day/week. Track via analytics SDK.
Target Benchmark: Varies by category. Social: 5-8 sessions/day, 3-5 min each. Utility: 1-2/day, <1 min. Shopping: 2-3/week, 5-8 min. Health: 1/day, 3-10 min.
Why It Matters: Session length × frequency = total engagement. Optimizing one at the expense of the other is usually wrong. Short frequent sessions signal habit; long infrequent sessions signal occasional utility.
Anti-Metric Warning: Don't optimize for session length in isolation — forcing users to stay longer (dark patterns, infinite scroll) increases time-in-app but may decrease satisfaction and long-term retention.

Keywords: app size, download size, install size, binary size, bundle size, storage, mobile app size
Definition: Download size of the app binary from the App Store / Play Store
Formula / Measurement: Check App Store Connect (iOS) or Play Console (Android) for download and install size. Use Android App Bundles and iOS App Thinning.
Target Benchmark: <50MB for download; <100MB for install size. Apps >150MB see significant download abandonment, especially on cellular
Why It Matters: Large app size kills conversion in emerging markets with limited storage and slow networks. Every 10MB increase reduces install rate by ~1% in markets like India, Brazil, SEA.
Anti-Metric Warning: Download size and install size are different — iOS App Thinning and Android App Bundles reduce download size. Always check the actual download size, not the project build size.

Keywords: deep link, universal link, app link, deferred deep link, attribution, install, open
Definition: Percentage of deep link clicks that result in the intended in-app action (for installed users) or app install + action (for non-installed users)
Formula / Measurement: (Users who completed intended action via deep link) / (Total deep link clicks) × 100. Track via Branch, Adjust, or AppsFlyer.
Target Benchmark: Installed users: 60-80% completion; Non-installed (deferred): 15-30% install + complete
Why It Matters: Deep links are the bridge between web/email/social and your app. Poor deep link handling means users land on the wrong screen or get stuck — killing conversion from your highest-intent channels.
Anti-Metric Warning: Don't count 'app opened' as conversion — measure whether the user reached the intended content/action. Deferred deep links (install → open to correct screen) need special tracking.

Keywords: uninstall, remove, delete, churn, mobile churn, app removal
Definition: Percentage of installed users who uninstall the app in a given period
Formula / Measurement: (Users who uninstalled in period) / (Total installed users at start of period) × 100. Track via Firebase or MMP (Adjust, AppsFlyer).
Target Benchmark: <3%/month is good; 3-5% is average; >5% requires investigation. First 7 days account for 50%+ of uninstalls
Why It Matters: Uninstall is the ultimate churn signal for mobile — it's harder to win back than web churn (user must re-download). High early uninstall means onboarding or first experience failed. High late uninstall means core value eroded.
Anti-Metric Warning: Uninstall tracking on iOS is limited (no direct callback). Use Firebase or MMP silent push detection. Android provides clearer uninstall signals via Play Console.

Keywords: iap, in-app purchase, paywall, subscription, premium, monetization, purchase, mobile revenue
Definition: Percentage of users who encounter a paywall or premium prompt and convert to a paid subscription or one-time purchase
Formula / Measurement: (Users who completed purchase) / (Users who viewed paywall/offer) × 100. Track via RevenueCat, StoreKit, or Google Play Billing.
Target Benchmark: Subscription: 2-8% of prompted users; Higher for hard paywalls (10-15%) but with higher churn. Free trial to paid: 40-60%
Why It Matters: IAP is the revenue engine for consumer mobile. Paywall placement, copy, and timing are the highest-leverage conversion levers. A/B test relentlessly.
Anti-Metric Warning: High paywall conversion with a hard paywall often comes with higher churn — users who converted under pressure don't retain. Track D30 retention of converted users alongside conversion rate.

Keywords: widget, quick action, shortcut, 3d touch, haptic, home screen, lock screen, live activity
Definition: Percentage of users who interact with app widgets or quick actions on their device home screen
Formula / Measurement: (Users who triggered widget_interaction or quick_action event) / (MAU) × 100. Track via custom analytics events.
Target Benchmark: 10-25% of MAU for well-promoted widgets. Top utility apps: 30%+
Why It Matters: Widgets extend your app's presence beyond the app itself — users see your content without opening the app. High widget usage correlates with higher D30 retention and DAU/MAU.
Anti-Metric Warning: Widget usage depends on discoverability — if users don't know your widget exists, adoption will be near zero. Prompt during onboarding or after the user establishes a usage pattern.

Keywords: sync, background, refresh, data sync, background fetch, offline sync
Definition: Percentage of background sync operations that complete successfully without error
Formula / Measurement: (Successful background sync operations) / (Total background sync attempts) × 100. Log sync_start and sync_complete events.
Target Benchmark: ≥95% success rate; <90% requires investigation. iOS background fetch has OS-imposed limits that reduce frequency
Why It Matters: Users expect their data to be up-to-date when they open the app. Failed background syncs mean stale data and manual refresh — a broken feeling. iOS is more restrictive than Android for background execution.
Anti-Metric Warning: High sync success rate doesn't mean frequent syncs. iOS throttles background fetch based on usage patterns. An app opened once a week may only get 1 background fetch/day. Design for eventual consistency.
